channel.title: Transcripts: Oxide Computer Company
channel.description: YouTube video transcripts from Oxide Computer Company

item.title: Oxide and Friends 5/4/2026 -- AI in Computer Science Education
item.description:
AI is an existential topic for all aspects of education--for none more so than Computer Science. Bryan and Adam were joined by Kathi Fisler and Shriram Krishnamurthi, professors of Computer Science at Brown, to discuss their experimental introductory course that strongly incorporates agentic programming. What do students take away from their "smoke the whole pack" approach?
Notes: https://github.com/oxidecomputer/oxide-and-friends/blob/master/2026_05_04.md

item.title: Oxide and Friends 5/11/2026 -- The Tale of Reverso
item.description:
Oxide ships a rack scale system--how test the manufacturing of the backplane and switches? Previously we've been using a collection of sacrificial servers, but this was unwieldy, expensive, and unscalable--all big problems as we ramp up manufacturing to 100s a month! Enter "Reverso", an extremely simple test fixture, that uncovered an extremely complex bug.
Notes: https://github.com/oxidecomputer/oxide-and-friends/blob/master/2026_05_11.md
item.content:encoded:
YouTube: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uuvADD4ISBg

Transcript

Okay, we're going to try this. Can you hear us now? Yes, you say split brain and I say nobody can hear you. Isn't isn't that that's just like what the other half of the split brain thinks? Yeah, but nobody nobody nobody in chat can hear you. But that's just other everyone everyone in this room could hear me. I was not alone. Actually, I've got I'm joined the litter box is at capacity friends. Full house. It is we have a full house. We got >> Doubled our atoms, too. We doubled the atoms. We have finally Adam Saszki has joined us in the litter box along with frequent guest but never

in person man myth legend Matt Keeter. Hello. In the litter box. Are you able to hear him all of us Adam? All three of you, yes. Um and then not in the litter box as if that's perjorative. We've got RFK joining us. Robert Keith, how are you? Well, I forgot that it's Hubrispalooza right now and they're all doing their thing. They're all doing their thing. Yeah, no it is it's like the fire festival in here. It's just an absolute wreck with the with the the Hubrispalooza nuts. It was a

It was a really fun to have the Hubris team. The Hubris team's out all week including Matt and as a result Matt does not know which time zone his body is in. I'm very confused. Which is should be excellent. Um All right, we are we are here to talk to tell the tale of Reverso and did you how how much have you followed the tale of Reverso? You know what? I feel like you know, I talk about like the Hawking radiation of these things that I don't particularly understand these black holes that like do emit something and I get I get some of the

vibes but Reverso is something I'll just tell you what I know so you can so you can correct any misconceptions. I know it's something to do with hardware and something to do with manufacturing. It's something that sure has taken a lot of work. That's what I have. I actually think you're kind of wrong on the the last part of it. I think that the a of work, yeah, so I would say that uh Reverso I think is a classic Adam hardware engineer kind of a [laughter] thing. Okay, now I'm interested, okay. Yeah, exactly. Now you're at the interesting This is cuz I would say if anything Reverso is

deceptively simple. Okay. Um and is a is this is the kind of thing that you come up with. Like why can't we just and it's actually as it turns out which is both is both a good idea and then one that is as we have learned slightly fraught but for no fault of its own. So with with that tantalizing lead in I think you were talking about when did we Matt and I were talking earlier today and trying to remember when we kind of the first idea for Reverso definitely dates back to the the development of Sidecar. Yeah, I mean it was a long time ago,

like a really long time ago. The uh what happened was >> [laughter] >> We have. And what what happened was is Sidecar is as I'm sure people know a a ton of cables inside it. And it's also our switch just to be clear. >> [laughter] >> I you know, the switch part actually seems to work reliably all the time. The the cabling part does not. Um and I mean that's pretty pretty normal, right? You got to be able to

test those kind of things. But since our system is had is very difficult to access, we're like, you know, once we've got this thing all wired up, we see you know, failures in particular links, which is like the for this is all Ethernet, so you'll have one lane that just kind of you know, doesn't work and you're like, okay, well well uh what how do I figure out where this is? Is it inside the Sidecar and all the cables in there? Is it in the backplane? After the Sidecar? Is it both? Is it just intermittent? Like does this you

know, does it work at speed or does it work you know, at very low rates? I don't know. Okay, and we're looking at this thing and saying, "Okay, we can build this like massive test fixture to go and like test each [snorts] one of the cables and then install them and then like presumably when they're installed they're okay." Turns out that's not true. Um okay, how do we get the cables inside the sidecar? I was like, "Well, we can loop those back on each other with like a cable of themselves and just plug them into the back of the thing." All right, so but once we have this all installed into a rack when everything's been plugged in, you still have to test it. Like, "Okay,

well, how do I do that without having an entire rack full of gimlets?" Yeah. >> Which is really expensive. Like, really really expensive. And we can't just like have those in the test facility. That's ridiculous. No one We're not going to do that. So, I was like, "Okay, well, let's be Tom and I were like, "Okay, let's be really dumb and [clears throat] what if we just, you know, turn the signal around?" >> orange here is part of it. Yeah, sorry, go ahead. Please, continue. >> Turn it around and it just goes right back into the sidecar. If you plug this thing in like it's a gimlet um like it's

a compete sled and it just it just goes right back to the itself and it doubles the length, so like it probably won't work at line rate. Um probably can, actually, but um but, you know, that doesn't matter. We're just trying to see if it's broken. We like if if it's if the copper's broken, it doesn't matter. Like, right? So, whatever. We're just going to build this board and it's really it's the same width as a gimlet cuz it has to fit into a gimlet chassis cuz again, we're trying to be really cheap and dumb. Like, "No, no, we're not going to build its own fixture. We're just going to use the same thing that the gimlet goes in and just build more

of those." No. Like, simple. The simplest thing possible. The board's like maybe 2 in long and and it's, you know, the same width as a gimlet and it's got XM X connectors on it and then right after the XM X connector, normally where you would break these things out and goes into an Ethernet chip, it's like just this tiny little loop. It goes right back into the other pin, you know, and then you plug it in like a gimlet and our idea at the time was well sidecar will just, you know, see itself and link up and, you know, whatever. No problem. And so we all we

built it. Tom and I, you know, birthed this board and then and then no one cared for like 4 years. It is actually if you don't mind me asking like how hard I mean this seems like a pretty straightforward board because you're basically going to for the you're basically going to turn around every link and every link that comes in is immediately going to go out the other side. Yes, it's extremely simple. But in yeah, and it should be said that like the the reason that we can do this is because every compute sled gimlet now Cosmo that whether that's Milan based return based is connected to two

switches. So you actually have two connections. So you can just come in on one and go out on the other is is the the premise of Reversa. Exactly and so but it it was supposed to be just like the simplest possible thing ever. Um and then because we tried to make it fit into all the things that we had it had its own little like quirks as everything does, but generally speaking this is like a completely passive very very simple board.

Yes. And so okay, so you guys lay the board out and do we when did we actually fab I feel like we did fab Immediately. We yeah, we immediately made it. Right. All right, and then it sat in the factory for like 3 years. It was pretty awesome. We're like we're going to use it and it just like never never got around to doing it and then but then eventually >> Brad, did you know this was did you know this was going to be an airing of grievances? Like did you did you see this one coming? I yeah yeah I I I First of all what's I I would say at least 30%

of our episodes are an airing of grievances. So, yes, I mean, of course like not at all a surprise. Our most popular >> can't be mad. I just like I can't believe that we let it go that long. Like we some I feel like we abandoned it. It's bad. Like we've done it we've done it wrong. Um I feel like why I mean we in part because we um like what First of all, we were Reverso and correct me if if I'm misremembering, okay, but I mean it was really first thought of as a sidecar test apparatus more so than an entire cable backplane. Although we certainly had the vision of doing it on

the whole cable backplane. Um >> Yeah. I'm trying to remember like what why didn't we end up picking it up earlier? I don't know. Well, because we thought we could solve the cable backplane breakage issues in a different way and we were also concerned that but we had more concerns about the the rats' nest inside the sidecar because we did see a lot of failures in there that were were more easily addressed with just, you know, a loopback cable because plugs in directly to back sidecar. Then we started

we kind of mitigated the cable backplane issues in different ways. So, we're like, "Okay, you know, this is working, but really there's no there is no replacement for the the these full assembly system test unless you want to go and build 32 gimlets and like run everything to um validate the backplane once it's installed and the sidecars are in and all this. Like it that's kind of a difficult problem or it can or just an expensive one, right? And these boards are

really cheap. That it's just the material and the frame, which we have chassis for gimlets and sidecars or gimlets and cosmos. It doesn't really matter at that point. Um and we built this with the intention of like, "Hey, when we hit volume um this is going to matter because we're not going to be able to have these test flight gimlets as we were calling them for a long time. Maybe this is why it was it was it was a religious There's an insertion. There's also an insertion count like limit to the XMAX connectors, right? You can only like insert them 300 times before they before they break. They or they have

there's signal integrity issues. So, you're going to start seeing errors that actually aren't really errors. You just you have bad connectors on your test flight gimlets. But, those are expensive. So, why would I why would I burn out the these connectors on things that are, you know, valuable? I could just burn them out on this cheap little board that no one cares about. Like that's a way better solution in manufacturing than you know, something you, you know, love and care for. Yeah, and I think we got away with it, too, just because our volumes were like low enough. And also, we had like plenty of other problems. That's totally We had lots of other I mean, just

whenever you manufacture something for the first time, you're going to learn a whole bunch. And but we got to the point where sometime last year when things really took off, and now we really needed to scale manufacturing quickly. And there were a whole bunch of things that we needed to go do to do that. We really needed Reverso. This was no longer merely nice to have. And I feel like that was Okay, that feels like that was what middle of last year, maybe towards the end of last year that we're like, okay, this is this is now going to be really load-bearing for our ability to scale

manufacturing. Like if we don't do this, this is going to this is going to really impair impede the the volume of racks we can ship. Yeah, sure. My sense of time is completely messed up, but I >> [laughter] >> If you say that time, I'll agree with you. It sounds good. And oh, and then also the because Reverso needs to effectively is occupying a cubby slot, it it has no computer in there. So, it's definitely we do not want to ship with Reverso.

Um what What's the What's the story of the of the remove before flight kind of orange? Uh it feels like it'd be very hard to to ship a rack accidentally with these things. Uh what was the origin of that? Do you happen to know? I've done a bunch of those things. And there's a They're on the test flight gimmicks gimmicks, too, because they I mean, like as you say, they look the same. But you want that just that and we're like, "Okay, we have tape. We're just going to go ahead and put all this on there." But he had also for other things, like the keys, uh he had taken those um like little tags that they would put on the bottom of like F-14 missiles that

you pull tabs before they take off. You know what I'm talking about? Yeah, yeah. >> Yeah, it goes on on the keys for the rack, too. So, there's all sorts of stuff like that all over, which is kind of which is fun. Just to like, "Please get rid of these before we send this to somebody. They don't want to have this. This is not what they asked for." They did not ask for a rack full of Reversos. Um so, okay, so we start using it So, this is this is great. So, now we're going to use Reverso and and and the concept feels so uh so simple. Um and um Matt, is this where cuz I feel like you had to do some things initially just

to support this at all in software, because software is is a little surprised by this. So, one of the first problems we had was that we would get kicked off the network at our contract manufacturer's site, um through no fault of their own, whenever we activated the Reversos, because all of a sudden packets would come in to the tech port of the rack, loop around the Reverso, shoot back out the tech port on the front of the rack, and that is a really unpleasant thing to be doing to someone else's network. And so, their switches would then just turn off that port. And we would have to go send them

an email and be like, "Sorry about that. Please turn our port on again. We think we've got the configuration right this time." And it only took a couple of tries for that to actually work out. And so, how did is So, we needed to uh Was that changing the the SP on the sidecar to configure things differently? Yeah, exactly. So, the SP on the sidecar is responsible for configuring the network switch that runs the management network, and we have a lot of control about how ports map to VLANs and how everything is interconnected. So, we have a special image for just the Reversos that makes

the ports a little bit more compatible with the outside world by hiding the weirdness inside the rack. Yeah, interesting. And this is kind of in in hindsight, maybe this this was an indicator of things to come that the the world was not yet ready for Reverso. The world is was not yet ready for the same MAC address showing up on multiple ports, yes. Exactly. >> [laughter] >> It it's it admittedly Reverso is results in rather unexpected behavior in the rest of the world because it's like, "Well, hey, your

the thing that you sent over there, now it's over here." Um I think I've described I think I have been open in this podcast in the past about being kind of a network aphasic. So, every time Rye, our our network genius, talks about like loops and cycles in network being bad, obviously like I fall asleep immediately. But, I think I'm starting to understand how this would be very surprising to a network if things started kind of coming up in places they didn't expect. Yeah. It's also kind of funny. Like, in a manufacturing setting, like,

all this stuff that you're running on any piece of equipment is pretty much meant to be like break rules to test things. And so, when the rest of the network around you like freaks out, you're like, "Well, you know, it's not always my bad. Like, we're supposed to do this." Right. >> That's my feeling, though. But, we got this to a point where we were no longer getting kicked off the network at our contract manufacturer. >> [laughter] >> That was probably a year or so ago. Like, this was one of those things where we brought it up for testing and then kind of put it on the back burner for quite a while and we like, you know, we

could see we could send packets in, we could get packets out, we could use the Reverso to prove that the links were good, and we hadn't really productized that until much more recently when things got more complicated. Yeah, so things got more complicated because now we desperately needed Reverso. Um, and Adam, Suzuki, you had joined our our budding manufacturing software team. And I I think what you knew when being tossed into this inferno was that there there was a transient networking error. Do you want to describe what the error that that

Cuz this is where the the kind of the second Odyssey of Reverso begins. >> Yeah, so the the transient network error was um we have a software suite of test suite run on the rack um called rack test. Um, what we were seeing is we run the rack test service as we ordinarily would. Um and there are dropped TCP connections. So um Now, you should say where are you running rack test from? >> so each manufacturing station has a

desktop computer that's running running uh this driver. Uh connected to that computer over ether uh to ethernet we're connected to the sidecar tech ports. So, there's two uh RJ45 um ethernet ports in each sidecar. So, we have four cables between the station computer uh and uh the sidecar. The sidecar has the uh VSC 7448, uh which is the man the management network switch, which also sends through the uh

tech port traffic. The Tofino, the larger um you know, uh high-speed data network switch of PCIE connection to the scrimlet. So, that's the PCIe attached uh compute sled that can uh configures uh the sidecar. And then running on that, there's the the switch zone. So, it's the zone you uh for network management that's running the other end of this driver. So, or so this is the the the the rack test agent. So, so the behavior is you you spin up the

service on uh the station computer, you send the agent to the scrimlet, the scrimlet sends that to all other hosts if there were any. In this case, we're on the Reverso. And and what what are the what does that agent do? So, the Yeah. And and this is a rack that includes two sidecars, two scrimlets because you have to have two computers that control the FF computer that controls each of the sidecars. And then the the rest of the rack, ideally, is all Reversos. Yeah. So, good question. So, that's where I enter where I entered

or turned the story was uh starting to look at a piece of software that was already written, already existed, um but had these intermittent flips. So, we'd see uh so so rack test from the perspective of the station computer, you get a effectively a status report that says these things are up or they're down or what have you. Um and it would show that there are three networks in the uh Oxide rack, the uh ignition, so it's the the power network, uh the management network, and the high-speed data network. And what we would see is this process of

the agent or the driver deploying the agent, uh the agent collecting data, the test passing, um and then due to some inter- intermittent failure, uh everything tearing down, restarting, and and cycling through that process. So, the Looking inside, I mean, what it's basically doing is on the agent, um there's tests of each of the three networks. So, ignition um uh data network, management network. The I Ignore the ignition case for now, but management network and

um uh data network are basically doing a uh a ping-like test. So, we're sending out a multicast um uh multicast packet uh using the DLPI, so the data link programming interface to each of the uh network interfaces for um for each cubby and for each of the network types. So, the management network and the high-speed data network. Um and right, if you get your packet back, you know your link is up and you're good. But, somehow uh and so you

get your you So, you get your packet back. So, what the What is the path that that packet is going to take in the functional case? Yeah, so the in the functional case. So, So, let's start, I guess, in the high-speed data network. The switch zone has um or you know, runs the Oxide networking services. So, this is um Dendrite and Magmite and uh things maybe other folks on here can describe in more detail, but it's a set of drive drivers in the Illumos host on the Srimlet that is um applying appropriate headers and forwarding packets to the Tofino P4

programmable switch uh connected to through PCIE. Um So, in the data network case, you're you're going to send the packet over PCIE to the Tofino through this set of Dendrite drivers. It should go up or out, you know, out the Tofino port um up the cable backplane through the Reverso back down through the Tofino and then back in, you know, through that same set of drivers and um VLANs and um you know, set of software running in the

switch zone. The data network uh similar but different you go you know same dendrite drivers you go through PCIE to the Tofino. The Tofino has a port to the other chip the the management network switch the VSC7448 also is that same switch that we came in from through the tech port um but that receives packet go ahead. Yeah that'll become important later like that'll become important Check with the this is

a check off switch is that what you're going to say? This is called foreshadowing. >> [laughter] >> So you're back in through the VSC's 7448 that does some VLAN tagging. Then goes out to each of the cubbies through the management network comes back in back through the Tofino back to the switch zone through dendrite and friends to your you know application software that is running in in that switch zone. Yeah one of the important things here is that the VLAN tags mean that we have essentially a

separate network for every cubby in the system. So packets leave the scrimlet with a particular VLAN tag and then the VSC7448 sends those packets to one of those cubbies based on the VLAN tag. And then when the reverse are populated those packets loop back around and reacquire the VLAN tag associated with that cubby when they reenter the VSC7448. So this is testing a lot. We we want to ideally we are testing we the purpose is to test the links to to to verify that the cables are actually working. And so

Adam what you're seeing is this the that the the link test would be adequate would be fine. But then the entire kind of rack test would give up the ghost. Yeah. Is that right? That is right. Um Yeah and so at this point and and this is a system that I was fairly new to and like most of the components I was learning about as I went. So uh the questions like how to how to approach the problem or decide and so there's kind of the physical like representation so you have the station

computer, the sidecar, the scrimlet, the reverso. Um you have like a logical representation so that is it application software like rack test um it the rack test itself some of the host services like dendrite um uh the host OS like is it something in say um in the kernel um is it something in the SP so humility or hubris runs um the sidecar SP which configures a bunch of the networking rules. Um

is it like one of the subcomponents on one of one of these boards um like the Tofino, the VSC 7448? Is it like a physical phenomenon like is there something electrically or or is it connector issue with the board? I also this particular rack under test it was uh remote to us so it was at a contract manufacturer site so you couldn't I mean you could ask somebody to go and and uh manipulate it for you but uh there were a wide range of both physical components and like logical components

that where the problem could be. Yeah, this is I mean rapidly turning into more murder on the Orient Express for you and and you are as you're learning about the system cuz I mean you I mean you only I mean you've been at the company only a couple of months at this point when you were looking into this. And I I I'm sure you're thinking like how complicated can it possibly be to get a packet from the tech port to the switch zone and the answer as as you were learning is really >> [laughter] >> extraordinarily complicated. And I I mean it is Adam, did you did you

read Paddle to the Sea as a kid? No. Caldecott Award winner. This is a but this is one of these books I think must have had a very narrow shelf life because I I think that this is clearly I can like maybe someone else born in 1973 would get it but the so this was a the premise of Paddle to the Sea is that this Native Canadian makes a carving of a canoe called Paddle to the Sea and the it says I'm Paddle to the Sea and I'm going to the the ocean and basically set me back in the ocean on my way through

the St. Lawrence Seaway and this is great book about like this you know this this little carving of a canoe getting in kind of all this trouble and and and folks other kind of characters kind of putting it on its way. I feel that like a packet from through the tech port is like Paddle to the Sea. Like I am a packet. I'm going to the switch zone. If you find me, please set me back on my way. It is a it it cuz it just you you go through so many different complicated paths anyway to get there. It's it's amazing that it

works at all. But Adam, I'm not sure if you're if you were having the same reference for it as you were learning about it. I had the same thought may not the same reference but >> [laughter] >> So Josh had left me a like a very useful debugging tool for this which he had um called net what and he had one commit in the in this repository which is psi. >> [laughter] >> Um And that that was kind of my next like breadcrumb of to start to go off of. Um

the Is this like the opposite of I have a truly marvelous proof that this martian is supposed to contain that? This [laughter] is like I have an unknown commit to net what with the commit message psi. So it was a smaller it was trying to get to a smaller reproduction of the behavior. So I what what this program would do would you know rather than running the entire rack test agent it was a connector and a listener so there was a part of it that ran on the scrimlet and then part of it that ran on the

station computer. The scrimlet side listens for incoming TCP packets on a port and echoes them back. The the sender side attempts to send 10 packets per second um listens for them coming back if the round trip time exceeds 1 millisecond it prints a line and if it is under 1 millisecond it you know prints the number of packets you've received consecutively. So in a healthy network you should

you know we have like direct connections between I mean there maybe are more things than we expected but still nothing that should contribute a millisecond of latency and what we saw was intermittent elevated round trip times typically around 400 milliseconds. Which makes no sense. Uh well Yeah Yeah. >> [laughter] >> Well yeah. Um one like so the the min RTO so the retransmit timeout what is 400 milliseconds. Right. >> And so that that was like okay well

there's some packets getting dropped we retransmit and then this like this phenomenon happens you know periodically. Yeah. Um but um >> is really surprising is really surprising in that it's like this is not going you know out to sea like this boat right this is sort of all right in front of you right like when you It's all in front of you. Like when Right. >> I was flying the other day and my ping time to the office was 400 milliseconds. I'm like, well, that's that sounds amazing, right? That that I live in the

world, right? >> in an aluminum tube going at at at 40,000 ft traveling at 600 mph. Like that seems reasonable. Yes. I like I can understand why some packets might lose their way here and there. However, this is not that. This is not that. No. And so we Yeah, we're clearly getting drops somewhere, but only when Reversos is running. Is that right, Adam? Yeah, so only when running the um the Reversos agent on the scrimlet, do you do

Well, actually I should I should You see elevated um uh uh instances or or higher frequency of high RTTs in that case, but you still see some even when the the Ractus agent is not running. But if if there are no Reversos in the rack, though, Yeah, yeah, so Yeah, so one uh thing I asked at our CM was one can you take all Reversos out uh and then do we have a problem? Uh yes, so we are clean in this case. And I said, can you put one Reversos in uh instead of 30? Yeah. Uh and it it would you'll you'll see a small a

small fraction of the el instances of high RTTs. So, the problem scales linearly with the number of Reversos that are populated. I'm [laughter] just clarifying. Which Yeah. It also like it it kind of it that also doesn't make sense cuz you're kind of like you're seeing what what where we're seeing these timeouts is like this feels like it should be at some level unrelated. Right? I mean, it's like you're putting Reversos in the rack and we are dropping kind of packets in and out of the rack over the tech port. Like how how does the rack know that a Reversos is plugged

into it? Yeah, and I mean, part of this sort of thing and then even the next couple debugging steps were that I given my unfamiliarity, like there's like what what's the information I want to debug? Like or what what would I want to do if I knew how to do anything? And then given what I know now, what would I do? And so it's actually very easy to say like, "Hey, can you Have you tried taking them out and have you tried just putting one in?" Were you pumping yourself like an LM? You're like, "Now Adam, act like an expert." And

I would say another thing is so small modifications to say net what or then I mean the next thing is to you know, we say say oh the the pathology is worse when the Ractus agent is running. So it's like, "Well, can I get tighter control of that?" So since instead of I I really I want to say, "Well, where where is the TCP packet getting dropped?" But um the sort like initially where I was heading was I can I get a more narrow like binary search the the behaviors and

then get like get a more narrow reproduction of of the issue. >> Yeah, right. Makes sense. And any any fruit there? There Yeah, there was some fruit. So so the main body of the the um uh Ractus agent is doing this ping test over the the two networks. And so and so that I mean that was one dimension is like the data network or the management network. um What I had seen So basically the the Ractus agent is pinging data network management network. um

Dendrite brings up each of the connections to sleds is either um uh you call the uh TF port rear, so the Tofino rear port or um one we name it a gimlet VLAN. So there's a number of VLANs for the management management network and then um uh Tofino drivers for each of the rear uh ports. And so as a matter of fact this is kind of where Matt jumped into and we were doing some experiments with behaviors on um on on those two networks. Yeah, so one

of the things that we tested was like, "Well, what if we just take all the reversos off of their VLAN?" So packets can go in but they don't actually go out on the reverso cubbies. And with that change in place, the system works fine. And we're like, "Oh great, we've solved the problem." And then we realized, "Oh we've just defeated the whole purpose." [laughter] I was just going to say, right. I just want to make sure I understood what you did. So that was a you you reversed reverso. So it was reverso became black hole, though. Is that right? Yeah, yeah, okay. Packets would go in directed to like cubby whatever and then they would just get dropped at the VLAN at the VSC

7448 cuz the VLANs would not include those ports. Right, but you have now no way of knowing if it's actually like if the link works or not. Yes, and we started considering desperate options at this point. >> Yeah, so that's like the bargaining part which Yeah, yeah, it's extremely [laughter] important, I think. I Desperation is a very important stage of debugging. Yeah, so so in this case you can ask the VSC 7448 um for the link status. Um and it can tell you the link is up. So it's not going to send uh packets back to the switch zone uh but

it will say up based on some information about it. Um Yeah, this is just like at the physical level, is there an SGMII signal that we're seeing on the link? And so I asked um folks more on the you know, mechanical side of or the operation side of uh manufacturing. It's like, "Hey, you know, we have this test and we can move migrate to the reverso test. We can get full test of the high-speed data network um and we can get you know, uh up status of the management network. Like is this you know, is that like is this you know, good enough?" And then I

mean the question it's like, "Well, how many you know, wires need to be pinched or like how damn how bad would it need to be for something to present as up and not, you know, uh uh support, say, TCP from the uh switch zone. And so, at that point, it's like, "Okay, no, we need to >> yeah, yeah, yeah, fine. Yeah, I you know, I I regret asking. Yeah, I I know. It It's like, "Wait, this is what whatever I mean, it's very helpful when you have an idea that like, "Hey, no, this is not a great idea." But then it's kind of helpful to have someone else like explore it really earnestly and realize like, "No, this is this is actually different. We need to merge it.

We need to actually need to go understand this goddamn problem." Yeah, and then so like the next steps were um I mean, a a similar experiment is uh in in the switch zone disabling all the the uh data network uplinks. Uh so, take all the rear ports down. Yeah. Uh and then and that uh basically has no no behavior change. So, if they're if things are good, it will remain good. If things are bad, it will remain bad. >> Okay. >> So, un- un- unlikely the like these rear All right, well, I shouldn't say that. Um

it was hard to know exactly what to be suspicious of. Like the the presentation of the different inter- So, this was like a misleading thread for me was the presentation of different interfaces in um the switch zone side. Um It's kind of how I got to the next point. So, I So, I I basically got to a point where um I I understood like how the frequency of pings required over a single management network loopback um in order to induce like a TCP disconnect Right. Okay. from the switch zone. And I had like very, you know, say, fine control

over that. And I'm like, "Well, this like the like VLANs seem relevant here because >> VLANs seem relevant and the management network seems relevant. Is there Is there something special about the management network? And the presentation of those interfaces from the switch zone is different in like a DLA DM output. And it's something like >> [snorts] >> Dendrite to me is also like this massive soft- software that I I'm not familiar with and um um um even prior to rack test, there were a few known bugs mentioned in the kind of that initial ticket and we were we were

running like a software version from that was from June, so it's like about eight months ago or eight months old at the time. And so I like some of my like well, maybe it is like something in this kernel side, dendrite side, some related to VLANs. Yeah. >> Yeah, we have a whole guide for debugging the management network which opens with it's not the management network's fault, which is something that I wrote about six months ago and that is called Hubris. >> [laughter] >> Because most of the time it's not the management network's fault. Like the management network is has been blameless in most of the cases where things are suspicious. It turns out that it's

something up stack. Yeah, that it's you know, in this Hubris lowercase H, not Hubris as >> not Hubris, the embedded operating system. >> Right. But Matt is not out here for a different Hubrispalooza. Oh, I'm sorry. I thought we were out here for the Hubrispalooza. [laughter] We we so the uh It kind of reminds me of the you know, when when Bonwick rewrote the kernel memory allocators way back in the day, Adam. And like if you rewrite the kernel memory allocator and this is like in this is in the mid-90s. And then and then ditched a bunch of custom allocators. Yeah, I mean every

kernel data corruption problem is now a bug in the kernel memory allocator cuz you always died memory allocator. And I mean Matt, I'm not unlike the management network. Things that Jeff ended up doing is adding a ton of very rich debugging support in the allocator so he could very quickly determine whose fault it actually was. And you've done the same thing. Like you had really rich debugging support. I mean the the management network and this is a little Dave Pachecoism about exonerating itself. The management network was very good at exonerating itself. Yeah, and the guy that I had written that started with it's not the management network's fault then went on to tell you how to

use all of these debug tools for like looking at port counters and looking at statuses and pinging things through different paths to exonerate the management network. And I I don't know if you Adam we we will need to get this some screenshots of some of the output of Matt's commands, but these things are like gorgeous. You're just like I almost want to have a a a problem with the management network just so I can revel in the glory of this thing explaining to me in detail that it's not its fault. Um It's really smart though because like presentation really matters. So when you when you are given this pristine output it's like how it's not my fault. What

are you going to do? Argue with that? Look at the ASCII art. Come on. >> saying I'm saying it's very well plated. I'm saying this is debugging output that's very well plated. Um But it's also just like very rich. Matt had built in a lot of really good debugging support. And in part because like you did have to frequently like the management network is where a symptom in something else will show up. Yeah, and it's also a relatively complicated chip. So like one of the tools we have is we we parse the CSDK doxygen comments to generate a bunch of register maps and then turn that into rust code and this

is all like horrifying horrifying code. But once we have that there's a sub command in Humility you can say like I just want to read a register from the VSC7448 by name and you can type in the name and it will look it up for you and then dump that memory and like reprint it for like pretty printed based on the registers field breakdown in the Humility debug info. And how just to give people a So even this thing would feel maybe like it's simple like oh I've got a this is in this is this industrial ethernet switch effectively which I think is where the 7448 is is typically used. But this is a wildly

complicated piece of hardware um and it How many registers are we talking about Matt? So the data sheet is 520 pages and uh it's a PDF that includes the registers as like an embedded PDF and the registers are 845 pages of documentation and that is not sufficient to actually bring up the chip. You then have to actually look at how it's done in the microchip SDK. In how many different uh instructions and architectures do we know that that are We've got to leave because there's like there's like a MIPS core on there, right, that we wire off? I think it's

just got the MIPS core and then it's probably got some cores hiding in the files somewhere. It but this is a really complicated and and Adam, I I we've got to ring the chime for our that the management network episode that we did um with Matt. I mean, what like 2 years ago now, maybe more, um going into just how complicated this thing is. It's only simple in comparison with the big switch, the Tofino, cuz Tofino is obviously a much bigger, much more complicated switch, but this one is I think it's 40-something ports and like 80 gigabits per second of switching fabric, so it's it's pretty hefty. It is

pretty hefty and just a reminder that all these I mean, that that the complexity of a switch is not merely its speed. You can have a switch switch that's at relatively lower speed, but it's is wildly complicated. And so when people look at that sidecar switch, it's got both the the Tofino and the 7420 A on there. Um Okay, so we are um we're looking at Matt's guide. We are we are learning that it can't possibly be the management network. We've got all these debugging tools to to go figure out what it actually is. And what what's next? >> Yeah, so next was um I sort of described my

controlled experiment over reproducing TCP connects to to Stu and Robert who are in the office uh at the time. Um and so I mean, in in contrast to the initial like when we run the rack test agent itself, it fails, like what we what we had now was when you connect net what um from the station computer to the switch zone uh and you know, concurrently um ping a management network link at the

kind of the threshold was a bit under um 500 milliseconds. You'll, you know, after a few, you know, some amount of time, maybe 10 seconds, get a disconnect in that TCP connection. Yeah. And I kind of explained to And there's a real solace in that reproducibility, by the way. Yeah, I mean, I have often said that bugs may be psychotic or non-reproducible, but not both. And we have a bug here, a classic This bug is psychotic, but it is reproducible. Yeah, and and it the

I mean, somewhat nice thing, too, is like the the connection that was breaking was uh disrupted by some completely you know, some other thing that you wouldn't expect. Like it wasn't a within the program itself. Like one thing um And so I explained a bunch of this to um Well, I actually I should say I explained a bunch of this to Robert, and he said, "Well, you should go check the VSC 7448. So, you should find the missing packet, and you should start in the VSC 7448." And I was like, "I don't know. I mean, well, I mean, I >> [laughter] >> What about like What about the Tofino? Or what about Dendrite? Like aren't like

these V like the VLAN designation in the switch zone like kind of suspicious that like the the you know, um links that induced the problem are like represented much differently in software than the management network links. We had one more thing which was suspiciously different between the Tofino and the management network, which was the distribution of MAC and IP addresses. Where on the main data network, I think every link had its own IP and MAC address, and on the management network, the scrimlet used the same MAC and IP address for all of the links to all of

the cubbies, and also to the tech ports. Yeah. And and yeah, Matt had like commented that right before, and was part of this whole uh payload I was describing to Robert. And Robert like It reminds me of the inner data corruption episode Adam you observing about the VAs being like I don't know are they on the they seem like these virtual addresses seem similar. And then so then Robert basically you know I drew the whole architecture diagram on the on the board for me so this is the tech ports to

the VSC 7448 through the Tofino you know through dendrite to the switch zone and like by the end of that I'm like oh it's obvious like obviously it's in the VSC 7448 like I like how could I be how could I be so naive yeah yeah right right yeah and then that's really where you know the pre-work that Matt had done for VSC 7448 was coming in um as soon like you know in hindsight a lot of things are >> [laughter] >> are everything makes perfect sense

but you know looking at the combination of the there was sort of a trail of three useful um VSC 7448 PRs related to Reverso the humility debug registers and then the 500 page data sheet >> Yeah. like if you come in with the mindset of like you know my goal is to find the missing TCP packet I'm looking for like a counter maybe that is awry um

I have duplicate MAC addresses like traffic that's going out and coming in has the same MAC address like >> Like I duplicate MAC addresses but that's by design. By design yeah so so so what Robert had explained on the board was like you know the role of VLAN tagging between the you know the switch zone the Tofino and VSC 7448 and um um the tag ports and access ports and the like expected behavior of the um

uh you know, of the of the VSC7448. Also, like if you now it's like ringing bells of like, okay, when when Eliza did this initial Reverso PR would for the uh the tech port loopback that was um uh causing us, you know, grief on the the uh the CM network like like now it's like, okay, all of this is like starting to make sense. >> Yeah. Um, and I've at that point it's like, well, I actually like had a notepad and I was like looking at, you know, the counters. I think I went to what I think it's RFD 144 has the whole uh switch

port uh mapping between VSC7448, the VLANs, um and the um um yeah, the to the Tofino and switch on um and um I mean yeah, so Monorail So, Monorail is the name for the management network. Uh Monorail status uh at least was, you know, showing the the ports we were uh ports we expect to have traffic having traffic, but then like really um looking at the you know, layer two

configuration of the VSC7448 was like now where we're getting richer information and at least words that when paired with the the you know, 500 page PDF when you go look at look at the data sheet uh it's like kind of screaming off the page. Yeah, so what was our next step? So, we've got we're we're we're beginning to kind of get a cordon around this thing. Um, what what was our the Was it the Was it the sticky bits next? >> Yeah, so I mean so one I'm like I have to give maybe some some There's >> [laughter]

>> There's a cloud code is actually pretty good at digging through your 500 page PDF say the the hubris source and the um our VSC 7448 source and you know, spinning out like the um useful and relevant registers. Yeah, interesting. So using Claude on the data sheet, which is kind of it was always kind of the the the I mean I the the fantasy for this stuff is like, can I give this this dense text and have it help me find some hypotheses? Yes. And

>> [laughter] >> in this case, yeah, I mean like I also have a the the graveyard of like uh hallucinated Because because actually if you say like this is there is a I was actually doing it right before this episode. I'm like, there is a problem in say the the Tofino in the Alumos, you know, driver for loop up. It it will give you like a an equally, you know, superficially plausible theory for like why you're seeing what you're seeing. So like so long as you point it at the right bug or like the right root cause, it it may, you know, it may find it, but Yeah,

interesting. It's it was helpful because now we you're looking for it's giving you some specific registers to look at for what kind of things? >> Yeah, so the like the the main what Matt was saying the the the sticky bits for local moves. So uh register the the chip will track when MAC addresses are moving between ports. And what we were seeing is all MAC addresses are moving between all ports. Which is like that also should ring bells because you know, what Matt had said is

all of these management network MACs is uh so for all of the scrimlets, for the power shelf controller, the sidecar, and the tech port tech ports from the perspective of the switch zone, yeah, have the same MAC and the same IP. And it's maybe worth taking a brief side track to talk about what MAC tables are doing in the switch. So when a packet comes into the switch, the switch is configured to do what it calls learning. And so it sees the packet's source MAC address and it sees the port that that packet came in on and it stores that information in a table and then next time when you try to send

a packet, if the destination MAC address is in that table, then it'll say, "Okay, this packet just needs to go out on port 12 to go to the the place with this source MAC address." And these If it doesn't see anything in the table, then it has to flood the packet on all ports until it eventually figures it out. But the port tables, the MAC tables in the VSC748 also include a field for the VLAN ID. So our assumption going in was, "Oh, the packets, even though they have the same MAC address, will be fine because they have different VLAN tags, so they won't end up colliding in the table and it'll learn, you know, this address on this port with this VLAN tag

is the upstream scrimlet and the same MAC address on one of these cubby ports has a different VLAN ID, so that's different." And so And then so when is it it believes this MAC is moving all around through different ports? How does that explain the the the dropped Cuz somewhere in this a packet is getting dropped. >> first thing or like the first change that I applied to the SP was to just turn off MAC auto learning. So when you

when you get a multicast packet in, when you when if you know the port for the for the corresponding MAC address, you'll send it to that port. If you don't, uh you you flood. You flood, right. >> Um and so if you turn off auto learn, you'll flood every time. Right. So okay, so with auto learning on, it's effectively sending it out to effectively the wrong port. Um Well, that's the question. >> [laughter] >> And it's like, well, are the Well, if the VLANs are isolate like I Yeah, depends on the configuration of the of

the chip. Um but basically we when we when we did turn off auto learning, actually the the pathology is is gone. Like, we're uh so like everything all reverse so test working. So, persistent TCP connected so you can ping your link and you can you can um you can ping ping the data network everything, you know, you know, problem solved. But and I have the same question as you, which is like but why why are TCP connections affected from the incoming tech port? And this is this is

when I was like, it's p.m. and I think this is working now, but I I'm like, now why does it work? Uh And at this point the the IFS iron is very firmly on the Mac table is not [laughter] behaving as we assumed they were behaving. Yeah. And And so, what's happening >> this is a so but but just learning that you can disable Mac auto learning and the the the pathological behavior goes away. That was an obviously a big breakthrough. >> Yeah, that was when I I shouted like, and you know, mark the time on the clock at p.m. as the the time was solved. I I do feel like Adam, I would love to

have like a I mean, if we could have omniscience for a moment, collect a sounds of debugging breakthroughs over the years [clears throat] and which I think are absolutely proportional to the amount of time that that you have worked on it. Um I had a bug that I'd been that had been I'd been chasing for so long that when I finally nailed it, I let out an absolute guttural howl and someone else in the office thought that quote someone had severed a finger. So, I think that if it sounds like you stayed within

>> [laughter] >> finger severing, it was more of a noting the time. I like that for like just posterity. But the the uh um but it this was a big I mean, obviously big breakthrough. Not all the way there yet, but it feels like a big breakthrough. We're going to live. Yeah. And so, I was excited excited about this. Um I yeah, and then really the next step is like yeah, why like why is this the fix and like in particular and why was it working? Like are there all like so many questions. A lot of questions. Um

and then in in Yeah, so maybe I'll explain why. I assume yeah, this TCP is getting disrupted because every time you have a you know, packet coming in from some source, the VSE 7448 uh you know, MAC table is associate it is making an association between this MAC and this port, but if the packet goes out and comes in, it's you know, very shortly after associated with a different port. Right. Um and for so also why why was the ping test

okay in the first place? Well, we're sending multicast packets from the um the switch zone. So behavior of that ping test is unaffected, but when the TCP connection comes in from the station computer through the tech port to the VSE 7448, um it's going to do a lookup of the MAC table uh for its destinate for that destination, which that's noted is the same as every other MAC for the you know, for each of the reversos.

So the question is like is is that your timing um for this MAC lookup in the time when you're in a invalid state where you're not going to get directed back into the to the switch zone. Which now is explaining like how come when we turn up the frequency of Reverso pings to the management network, like do you see it increased instances of either round trip times or eventually the the disconnect? Yes, so it's basically if a packet has come in through a Reverso cubby uh more recently than from the actual switch

zone, then any packet that you try to send to the switch zone gets sent to that Reverso cubby instead, and then it kind of falls off the edge of the map and you're having a bad time. Yeah, we we kind of I mean we we've definitely haven't been in pathological switch behavior. Although admittedly also like kind of pathological in Reversal itself is is um rather unsporting. Um in terms of what it's doing. Although this is where RFK would be like, "No, no, this is like sorry. This is This is doing exactly what it what it should be doing, which is uh testing the rest of the software stack." So, what was the the um is the

the the the proper fix is the is not merely to disable the the the MAC auto learning, but we'd actually So, didn't we we we missing a VLAN tag as well? Yeah, so the the proper fix was very dumb. Actually, this was one of the things that Claude picked out as like one of the suspicious registers. Uh so, remember you know, 20 minutes ago when I said, "Oh, the MAC tables use the VLAN information." It's true that they can use the VLAN information, and it's also true that by default they don't. So, the fix ended up being very dumb. We just had to turn on

a configuration bit for each VLAN that says, "Hey, by the way, also use this VLAN tag for MAC table associations." Yeah, wow, interesting. And that was uh Claude Claude pointed that out or suggested it. >> I think it it pointed at the right register, yeah. >> Yeah, that's interesting. Yeah, and then I I the question I had then too uh was So, if you put on VLAN aware MAC tables, then like why like why why is our ping test still unaffected because we still have this thrashing of the same

MAC address on the same port uh or or the same MAC address coming on uh different ports, that is, you know, port 49 associated with the Tofino, and then the outgoing port the uplink port to the Reversal Cubby. Yeah. And I mean it it's like the only thing we need to do is send a multicast packet and get it back. So, in this case, perhaps we are are thrashing our table, yeah, but you know, it doesn't matter because we we still get the packet back and we we know it's the one we sent. Yeah, interesting. Yes, the table still trashes for the cubby VLAN IDs because

those those appear from both the scrimlet and then from the reversos coming back in, but it doesn't thrash anymore for the switch zone, which is the thing we actually need for the TCP connections. Right. Yeah, interesting. And so, in completeness, you could say turn off the turn off tables for uh reverso base cubbies, keep it enabled for the others, um and in for all always ensure that we do VLAN tagging for uh traffic internal to the VSE. And so, then with this now properly fixed um we've got we can now run with

reverso with without issues. We can do the rack test and um with the case closed. >> Yeah, the basically um you know, there there was kind of the the the ticking the time bomb on this was as we we want to scale racks and the concurrency of rack chassis or rack chassis with the cable backplane is sort of this function of how many fully loaded uh gimlets do we have available for testing? And 16. Um so Yeah. um

if you want to build more racks at once and or even you know, if there if some of these test units fail, like what's the what do we do? So, yeah, this is the exciting break breakthrough because I mean for all like for cost uh for you know, um sort of operationally like there the weight of the sled like the reversos are you know, I don't know, 10% of the weight of a full cosmo like a lot of thing it it unblocks a lot of um We can just we can move the line a a faster, yeah. We can

move the line a lot faster. Um and I mean this was the our I mean and I we've been saying this for a little while, but we definitely are in the how fast can you make them problem. And as we tell ourselves like these are great problems to have, but they are problems. And this is a very thorny I mean you know one of the things that that it kind of highlighted for me is that well, one I mean we've said said this over and over again that like when you see these wisps of smoke they can come from a a coal fire surprisingly deep under the surface.

Adam, I don't know if I mean you ended up learning a whole lot about the the management network. >> Yeah, I thought it was it was it was an >> [laughter] >> effective onboarding experience. Like one of the the uh I like I kind of wrote some, you know, reflections of like what would I do differently or like what was I kind of like how would I you know, for the next mysterious bug like what would I do, but I think the hard part is um I mean part of like what what do I go read and study and like stop and go you know, take a look at versus when to like actively probe the system. And I

think I don't know, it's even in hindsight it's hard to know because I think the the volume of things I could have gone and learned and read at a given time was I likely that I would have read and looked at the wrong things. So I think like the active learning part was especially useful. Yeah. Um but then I mean I think getting recommended reading from from like I think you know, Stu had sent me like the most relevant part of probably the most relevant RFD about 12 hours

before the break. So I think this like you know, probe what you can, like share this information and then like ping some people who know what they're what they're doing. Yeah, I did I did like you there is a bit of a Where in the World is Carmen Sandiego kind of property to this where you were kind of going around being like know, the the the the the the the packet mentioned something about the 7448. And if and then uh went to the airport. But the um kind of being able to pull from I think also like just knowing that it's all knowable. Like we do actually control this whole thing. Uh you know, asterisk um because we are

dealing with ultimately these these hardware parts that can have surprising behavior. >> Well, well, Brian, I mean I know that this is not uh this sort of not the thrust of that, but what you said is really important. Like that we can understand this whole thing. And part of the reason we built this hardware and software together was so that we could understand the whole thing. So that when customers encounter some problem, we're not just chasing the problem to the next vendor over, but like we actually can debug this thing. And I think sometimes that gets lost a little bit in the like hardware software co-design cuz we enable lots of great stuff. But in particular, one of the

things we enable is being able to resolve problems independently. Yeah, absolutely. And I think that you this is yet another I feel like we've had many of these vivid embodiments of it, but like this is another vivid embodiment of if we didn't control these components, at some point we would have just given up and been like, this is a weird mystery. We don't get it. We're shipping it. And and the and the good is it I mean this was a relatively in terms of like the customer impact. This is just this is an issue that does have customer impact. Um and we did discover some things along

the way where we um I mean we we we made it ultimately made the product better, not just by by making it worse or better. Yeah, I mean this is not a thing that you would see under normal operation, but if someone was trying to poison our MAC tables maliciously, then having the VLAN tags adds another layer of protection there. Right. Um and uh and I think just in general having like having an opera you could you could have an operation on the tech port that would I mean this is this cleans up our own understanding even though we don't

intend any reversos we plugged into production. Yeah, in hindsight it's extra it's so it's obvious. Like I and then it's like well, why didn't why didn't I look there sooner and even if you pull up the data like pulling up the data sheet uh you know, you look at this 500 page PDF and it's it's like it's screaming off the like page 11 it's like right there uh and yeah, it's I mean yeah, I think it is powerful like going in knowing like I can I can find this and like it it is a this is like a deterministic

reproducible behavior. Like I have all of the or you know, most of like all of the there's quite a lot of source that we control and like we can we can find it. We can find it. Well, and it and and then this is as it turns out like a big unlock for scaling manufacturing which has been exciting and I mean Adam you you joined the you definitely have not wanted for lack of things to do at Oxide with the manufacturing software team which

has been grown quite a bit over the last couple of months. Well, this is awesome. Great to have you both physically in the water box. The enjoy the rest of Hubris Palooza Matt. Um Thank you. And we will have an out loud reading from the the the the network that the management network is not the problem. Um as part of Hubris Palooza but this is uh I'm sure this was the last bug in the management network. >> This was yeah, exactly. Good for now. Yes, exactly. There was a bug there was

an issue with the management network but it's now been found and fixed. Um but I think it actually it does highlight how important it is to have these really robust components and the debuggability of them to allow us to And so hey RFK nice going on Reverso. Do you feel like as Do you feel that you're kind of the creator of chaos here? I mean, this must feel great to watch all of this. That's just the the way hardware engineering like we just make all your problems and then give it to you and think, "Okay, well, they'll just go figure that one out." There you go. >> go. You're welcome.

Exactly. Thank you. Thank you for Reversao. Um and uh and yeah, the Reversao itself has been great. I think that Eric in the chat is saying it's worked better than we anticipated. I think it's been a big big win. Um so uh that that's been I'm glad it works. You know, like at the time it got a lot of hate cuz we're like, "Why would we ever need this?" Cuz it was like year one or two and we have no product. It's like we don't need this manufacturing scale thing and ah Now we do.

Yeah, I I I don't regret trying to scale manufacturing ahead of our actual demand because it the or rather I don't regret having not It's so complicated to scale manufacturing. You actually don't know the problems you're going to have until you're actually like right in the thick of it, I think, in a lot of this stuff. Um but um it's been fun and uh yeah, looking forward to uh or the the next bottleneck and Adam, the next thing you hit, you know you can you know you know you can >> I'm excited for it. That's it. >> [laughter] >> That's true. You know, I can cut that from the recording if you want. That's right.

Yeah, he wants to reserve judgment on that. Um awesome. All right, well, um thanks you two. Um and RFK, thank you so much for Reversao. Um and Adam, thanks for uh thanks for getting over your network aphasia to join us on this one. This This is a good one. Yeah. All right, awesome. Thanks, everyone. Take care. See you next time.

item.title: Oxide and Friends 6/1/2026 -- This Old Repo: LLMs and the Restoration of BattleTris
item.description:
Bryan and Adam discuss the process of restoring a software project--BattleTris--untouched and unbuilt in over 20 years! How did LLMs help restore code Bryan started in the mid-1990s and what does that teach us about developing and maintaining software in the future?
BattleTris: https://github.com/bcantrill/BattleTris Notes: https://github.com/oxidecomputer/oxide-and-friends/blob/master/2026_06_01.md
